text: The surgeon begins the ambulatory supracervical hysterectomy by detaching the lateral attachment of a large polimiomatous uterus. The procedure starts with cutting the left round ligament using a harmonic scalpel. Next, the mesosalpines are cut, and a salpingectomy is performed, sparing both ovaries. The fallopian tube is cut close to the tube, away from the ovarian pedicle, to preserve ovarian vascularization.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to achieve hemostatic control before cutting the adnexal pedicle, progressively releasing the left ovary laterally. The procedure begins with the lateral aspect of the uterus to ensure a clear view of both uterine pedicles, and the same steps will be repeated on the opposite side.

The surgeon cuts the right round ligament and the fallopian tube, while controlling the utero-ovarian pedicle close to the uterus. Tissue is coagulated at a level of two centimeters, followed by precise cutting in the middle to ensure a dry dissection and minimize ureteral injury.

The surgeon increases uterine mobility and cuts the posterior leaf of the broad ligament towards the upper part of the uterosacral ligament, carefully avoiding the clearly visible uterine vein. The dissection continues with the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, with the harmonic device positioned just above the large uterine vein. The vesico-vaginal peritoneum is tracked and cut, maintaining a dry surgical field, providing a clear view of the vaginal area.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine pedicle to apply bipolar energy directly on the vessel, avoiding the surrounding peritoneum. Coagulation is applied over a three-centimeter area at the isthmic region. The right uterine artery and vein are controlled using bipolar energy, and the uterus is pulled to the left. The surgeon then cuts the uterus at the isthmic level using the harmonic device, performing a supracervical hysterectomy without a uterine manipulator.

The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle using bipolar energy and cuts the pedicle with cold scissors, ensuring no hemorrhage. The supracervical hysterectomy is completed in a bloodless manner. The right fallopian tube is removed to decrease the risk of residual ovarian cancer.

The surgeon identifies and controls the right ureter, which is clearly visible. The ureter shows rotation. An ovariopexy is performed using a Vipril-O suture, employing an intracorporeal knotting technique to secure the ovary and reduce the risk of ovarian torsion, especially in the presence of a large uterus.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy on the opposite side using a large needle to facilitate the procedure and locks the knot with a specific technique. The ovary is visibly well-positioned. The surgeon then inserts a 15mm morcellator into the left iliac fossa, with a setup of 700 RPM, to begin morcellation of the uterine tissue, which weighs 700 grams. The morcellation process takes approximately half an hour.
This is the final aspect of our surgery. I hope this video was useful for my young colleague. Thank you for your great attention.
